coredata.dc:title: A fluorescent nanoprobe based on N/S co-doped carbon dots coupled with molecularly imprinted polymers for the detection of curcumin
coredata.dc:creator: Fang, Fang
Zhang, Shaohua
Chen, Chaofan
Wang, Xueying
Luo, Chuannan
Wei, Qin
coredata.dc:description: Curcumin can be used to treat cancer diseases, however the side effects of excessive use are serious, and the dosage of curcumin should be accurately controlled. In this study, a fluorescence nanosensor using carbon quantum dots (N,S-CQD) embedded multifunctional molecularly imprinted polymer (N,S-CQD@MIP) composite was developed as a probe for the detection of curcumin. The N,S-CQD@MIP was synthesized by sol-gel method and found a maximum emission peak at 430 nm. The curcumin can be binded selectively by the specific interaction between recognition sites and template molecules. The corresponding sensing mechanism of N,S-CQD@MIP for the detection of curcumin is the result of fluorescence inner filter effect and the static quenching effect. The fluorescence nanosensor using N,S-CQD@MIP for the curcumin detection has a good linear relationship in the range of 0.339–136.0 μM, and the corresponding detection limit is as low as 1.357 nM. This method has been applied to detect the curcumin in real samples with the recoveries of 97.6–103.7%. These results indicate that N,S-CQD@MIP are potential sensing materials for curcumin detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence
Carbon quantum dots
Molecularly imprinted polymer
Curcumin detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The preparation of N,S-CQDs@MIP and N,S-CQDs@NIP composites
Optimization of determination conditions for Cur using N,S-CQDs@MIP
Effect of the quality of N,S-CQDs@MIP
Stability and the ability of anti-interference
Content of Cur consumed by cells in 2 h

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of N,S-CQDs (scale bar:10 nm); (b) FT-IR spectra of N,S-CQDs; (c)Absorbance, excitation and emission spectra of N,S-CQDs; (d) Fluorescence spectra of N,S-CQDs with and without Cur.

SEM image of N,S-CQDs@MIP (a) and N,S-CQDs@NIP (b)(scale bar:300 nm); FT-IR spectra of N,S-CQDs(Ⅰ), N,S-CQDs@MIP(Ⅱ) and N,S-CQDs@NIP(Ⅲ) (c); Fluorescence emission spectra of N,S-CQDs@NIP, N,S-CQDs@MIP before and after template molecules extraction (d).
(a) XRD patterns of N,S-CQDs@MIP; (b) Effect of pH value; (c) Effect of the quality of N,S-CQDs@MIP; (d) Adsorption curve of N,S-CQDs@MIP for Cur.
(a) The calibration curve of N,S-CQDs@MIP and N,S-CQDs@NIP; (b) The stability of N,S-CQDs@MIP after adding Cur; Effects of common drugs (c) and metal ions (d) on the N,S-CQDs@MIP.

(a) Absorption spectrum of Cur, excitation and emission spectra of N,S-CQDs; (b) The absorption spectra of N,S-CQDs, Cur, N,S-CQDs and Cur; (c) Quenching curves at different temperatures; (d) Fluorescence decay curves of N, S-CQDs with or without Cur.
Schematic diagram of synthesis process of N,S-CQDs@MIP
Comparison of different analytical methods for detection of Cur.
Recovery rate of curcumin capsules by standard addition.

head.ce:title: A fluorescent nanoprobe based on N/S co-doped carbon dots coupled with molecularly imprinted polymers for the detection of curcumin

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Interfacial Reaction & Sensing Analysis in Universities of Shandong, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan, 250022, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Interfacial Reaction & Sensing Analysis in Universities of Shandong
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Interfacial Reaction & Sensing Analysis in Universities of Shandong, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan, 250022, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Curcumin can be used to treat cancer diseases, however the side effects of excessive use are serious, and the dosage of curcumin should be accurately controlled. In this study, a fluorescence nanosensor using carbon quantum dots (N,S-CQD) embedded multifunctional molecularly imprinted polymer (N,S-CQD@MIP) composite was developed as a probe for the detection of curcumin. The N,S-CQD@MIP was synthesized by sol-gel method and found a maximum emission peak at 430 nm. The curcumin can be binded selectively by the specific interaction between recognition sites and template molecules. The corresponding sensing mechanism of N,S-CQD@MIP for the detection of curcumin is the result of fluorescence inner filter effect and the static quenching effect. The fluorescence nanosensor using N,S-CQD@MIP for the curcumin detection has a good linear relationship in the range of 0.339–136.0 μM, and the corresponding detection limit is as low as 1.357 nM. This method has been applied to detect the curcumin in real samples with the recoveries of 97.6–103.7%. These results indicate that N,S-CQD@MIP are potential sensing materials for curcumin detection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Curcumin (Cur), an edible natural pigment with diketone structure derived from the rhizome of Curcumalonga L, is widely used in food production [1]. In addition, Cur is commonly used to treat diseases due to its pharmacological activities [2], including anti-inflammation [3], antioxidant [4], lowering blood lipids [5], anti-atherosclerosis [6] and anti-tumor [7]. The anti-cancer activity of Cur has been demonstrated in many studies that the proliferation of tumor cells can be inhibited by restrained the expression of inducible nuclear factor kappa B (NF-κB) [8]. However, many evidences also demonstrate that Cur may cause DNA damage of normal cells at effective concentration (5–50 μM) [9]. Therefore, the development of sensing strategies for accurately detection of Cur is very important in the fields of life sciences.

Nowadays, there are many methods for the quantitative determination of Cur with high accuracy and sensitivity, such as high-performance liquid chromatography [10], capillary electrophoresis [11], electrochemical [12] and so on. However, the cumbersome sample pretreatment, complex operation and unstable electrodes limit their application. Meanwhile, fluorescence spectrometry has attracted extensive attention with advantages of convenient operation, high sensitivity and fast response [13]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a zero-dimensional nanomaterial, with remarkably biocompatibility, low toxicity, excellent water solubility and outstanding optical performance [14], has been widely used in the fields of bioimaging, photocatalysis, chemical and biological sensing. Hu et al. [15] reported a sensing mechanism methods for Cur detection with detection limit of 38 nM based on nitrogen and chlorine co-doped CQDs. Tang et al. [14] constructed a Cur detection probe based on nitrogen-doped fluorescent carbon dots, and the detection limit was 29.8 nM. However, the selectivity of fluorescence method based on CQDs needs to be improved, and molecularly imprinted polymers combined with carbon dots is proposed to solve this problem.

Molecular imprinting polymers (MIPs) have properties similar to biological antibodies and often synthetized by copolymerization in the presence of template molecules [16]. The obtained cavity structure on MIPs only allows the target molecule to re-enter the MIPs after removing the template, which can increase the selectivity [17]. Therefore, an analytical method with highly selective and efficient fluorescence characteristics can be developed by combining MIPs with CQDs [18,19]. A fluorescent sensor based on MIPs/CQDs for the detection of indoxacarb with 1 nM detection limit had been developed [20]. Hu et al. established an optical sensor by encapsulating Mg, N-CDs into MIPs and used for detecting tetracycline, and the detection limit was 0.79 ng mL−1 [21].

In this work, a fluorescent sensor for the detection of Cur was developed based on N,S-CQD@MIP. Firstly, CQDs with blue fluorescence were fabricated by modified hydrothermal method utilizing citric acid and o-phenylenediamine.

The N,S-CQD@MIP were synthetized by sol-gel reaction. Cur and 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES) were stirred in ethanol solution to form pre-polymer. Then N,S-CQDs solution, tetraethoxysilane(TEOS) and NH3·H2O are added to generate the N,S-CQD@MIP through stirring. The products we need are obtained by eluting Cur (template molecule) and vacuum drying. Combined the advantages of CQDs and MIPs, the fluorescence method for the detection of Cur based on N,S-CQD@MIP with greatly sensitivity and superior selectivity was built. The quenching mechanism of quantum dots was discussed by ultraviolet spectroscopy, fluorescence lifetime and the influence of temperature on K SV. After a series of characterization and conditional experiments, the optimal conditions for the detection of Cur were obtained and the Cur in real samples was detected successfully.

Absolute ethylalcohol, Quercetin (QR), Phenacetin, Docetaxel were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Reagent Co. Ltd. Tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), Acetic acid, Tetracycline, Amoxicillin, Adriamycin hydrochloride (DOX) were all purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co. Ltd. Vancomycin injection was purchased from Korea CJ HealehCare Corporation. Artemisinin was purchased from Shanghai Sun Chemical Technology Co., Ltd. Absolute methanol and Acetonitrile were all purchased from Tianjin Fuyu Fine Chemical Co., Ltd. All chemical reagents of analytical grade were used without further purification.

The absorption spectrum was measured by dual-beam UV–Vis spectrophotometer TU-1901 (Beijing Puxi General Instrument Co., Ltd.). Fluorescence spectrum was obtained by a PELS55 fluorescence spectrophotometer (American PerkinElmer Corporation). The FT-IR spectrum was recorded by FT- IR spectrometer VERTEX 70 (Bruker Company, Germany). The transmission electron images were obtained by Tecnai F20 transmission electron microscope (FEI of the Netherlands) with scientific compass. The freeze dryer was from Ningbo Xinzhi Biotechnology Co. Ltd. The fluorescence lifetime was measured by the combined FLS920 transient/steady fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instrument Company, UK).

The N,S-CQDs were obtained using the reported hydrothermal method [22]. As displayed in Scheme 1 (a), Briefly, 0.2 g l-cysteine and 0.2 g dehydrated trisodium citrate were added into 10 mL water and 500 μL of anhydrous ethylenediamine was slowly added into the above solution. Then, the mixed solution was sonicated and dispersed for 5 min, then putting the mixed solution into a Teflon lined reactor to react at 200 °C for 4 h then cool to room temperature. Next, carbon dots solution was obtained by filtering the mixture with 0.22 μm membrane to remove larger particles, the final product was stored at 4 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The preparation of N,S-CQDs@MIP and N,S-CQDs@NIP composites
ce:section.ce:para: N,S-CQDs@MIP was prepared by modified previously reported methods [23]. As displayed in Scheme 1(b), 10.0 mL absolute ethanol with 1.0 mg Cur and 0.15 mL APTES were stirred at room temperature for 30 min, then 5.0 mL N,S-CQDs solution, 0.20 mL TEOS and 0.10 mL NH3·H2O(25%, w/w) were added into the above mixed solution, and the mixture solution was stirred for 12 h at room temperature. The obtained N,S-CQDs@MIP product was centrifuged and washed repeatedly with acetic acid/methanol (v/v, 1/9) mixture solution until the Cur was not detected in the supernatant. Eventually, the N,S-CQDs@MIP was obtained after drying at 60 °C for 24 h, and N, S-CQDs@NIP was prepared using the same method without Cur.

The fluorescent measurements were performed with a PELS55 fluorescence spectrophotometer under the excitation wavelength of 360 nm, excitation slit widths of 5 nm and emission slit widths of 15 nm. Different concentrations of Cur were added into 0.8 mL of PBS buffer solution (pH 7.0) with 1.6 mg of N,S-CQDs@MIP (N,S-CQDs@NIP). FL emission spectra of N,S-CQDs@MIP with Cur were recorded after reacting for 30 min.

The fluorescence nanosensor based on N,S-CQD@MIP for the detection of Cur in real samples was studied. Cur in curcumin capsule was dissolved with absolute ethanol, and then mixed and reacted with 2.0 mg mL−1 N,S-CQDs@MIP solution for 30 min. Cur concentrations were calculated by fitting the fluorescence quenching performance F 0 /F to the working curve and sample recoveries were determined by spiked recovery method.

The content of Cur consumed by A549 cells was also calculated by a similar method: A549 cells with a density of 1 × 104 N/mL were planted in 96-well plate. After cultured for 24 h, the cell culture solution was sucked out and 200 μL PBS solution was added, then Cur with different concentrations were added into the 96-well plate and incubated for 2 h. The PBS solution with residual Cur was mixed with 2.0 mg mL−1 N,S-CQDs@MIP solution and reacted for 30 min. The consumed Cur concentration by cells was calculated by fitting the fluorescence quenching performance F 0 /F to the working curve.

The morphology and sizes of the N,S-CQDs were characterized by TEM. As shown in Fig. 1 (a), the average sizes of the N,S-CQDs are 2.5 ± 0.4 nm, and N,S-CQDs are spherical with good dispersion. The surface functional groups of the N,S-CQDs were analyzed by infrared spectroscopy and the results are shown in Fig. 1(b). The broad and intense absorption bands around 3410 cm−1 and 2978 cm−1 correspond to O–H and N–H stretching vibration, an obscure absorption peak near at 2400 cm−1 is mainly contributed to S–H stretching vibration, the absorption peaks at 1660 cm−1 and 1401 cm−1 correspond to CO and C–N stretching vibration. The characteristic absorption bands of C–S at 1293 cm−1. These absorption peaks in infrared spectroscopy symbolized that N,S-CQDs has been synthesized successfully.

The excitation, emission and absorption spectrum of N,S-CQDs were recorded in Fig. 1(c). Obviously, the emission peak of N,S-CQDs is 442 nm when excited at 350 nm, due to the π-π* transition between the CO and CN bonds of the N,S-CQDs. The aqueous solution of N,S-CQD is a pale yellow transparent liquid under natural light and emit a light blue fluorescence under UV irradiation. After the addition of Cur, the fluorescence intensity of the N,S-CQDs is significantly reduced (Fig. 1(d)), indicated that N,S-CQDs could be used as fluorescence nanosensor to detect Cur.

The morphologies of N,S-CQDs@MIP and N,S-CQDs@NIP microspheres were determined using SEM(Fig. 2 (a) and (b)).The particle size distribution histogram in Fig. 2(a) illustrated that the sizes of N,S-CQDs@MIP are around 350 nm, and the TEM image of N,S-CQDs@MIP is showed in Fig. S1. Obviously, the sizes of N,S-CQDs@MIP (N,S-CQDs@NIP) are much larger than of the N,S-CQD. The FT-IR spectra of N,S-CQDs (Ⅰ), N,S-CQDs@MIP (Ⅱ) and N,S-CQDs@NIP (Ⅲ) were shown in Fig. 2(c). For curve Ⅰ, the characteristic absorption peaks at 3410 cm−1 (O–H), 2978 cm−1 (N–H), 2389 cm−1 (S–H), 1579 cm−1 (CO), 1489 cm−1 (C–N), 1332 cm−1 (C–S) repersent the existence of nitrogen and sulfur in the N,S-CQDs. Compared with curve Ⅰ, the characteristic absorption peaks in curve Ⅱ and curve Ⅲ at 1120 cm−1 and 1070 cm−1, the stretching of Si–O–Si and Si–O–C, illustrated the formation of MIPs doped with N,S-CQDs.

The synthesized N,S-CQDs@MIP can be used to Cur detection sensitively. As illustrated in Fig. 2(d), before washing off the template molecules of Cur, the fluorescence of the polymer was quenched vastly, after washing off the template molecules, the fluorescence intensity of the polymer recovered to N,S-CQDs@NIP approximately. Fig. 3 (a) displayed the XRD pattern of N,S-CQDs@MIP, having two broad diffraction peaks centered at 6.12° and 23.84°, which confirmed the amorphous nature of N,S-CQDs@MIP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of determination conditions for Cur using N,S-CQDs@MIP

ce:section.ce:para: The recognition ability of N,S-CQDs@MIP for the detection of Cur at different pH value was investigated (Fig. 3(b)). The results showed that the fluorescence quenching competence of N,S-CQDs@MIP increase with the increasing of pH value from 4.0 to 7.0, and then showed a slight decrease from 7.0 to 11.0. The reason for this behavior may be that the binding sites of N,S-CQDs@MIP were protonated and deprotonated. Therefore, 7.0 was chosen as the best pH value for the further experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of the quality of N,S-CQDs@MIP
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 3(c), with the concentration of N,S-CQDs@MIP increasing from 0.5 mg mL−1 to 5.0 mg mL−1, the fluorescence quenching ratio F 0 /F has different trends while 2.0 mg mL−1 has the maximum fluorescence quenching ratio. In order to obtain stable fluorescence intensity and high sensitivity, the optimum concentration of N,S-CQDs@MIP to detect Cur chose 2.0 mg mL−1.

The adsorption curve of N,S-CQDs@MIP to Cur was shown in Fig. 3(d), with the increasing of Cur concentration, the fluorescence intensity decreased gradually. When Cur concentration reached 62.5 μg mL−1, the fluorescence intensities were almost unchanged, so the maximum adsorption capacity of N,S-CQDs@MIP to Cur was 62.5 μg mL−1(3.125 mg/g, w/w).

With the increasing of Cur concentration from 0 μg mL−1 to 50.0 μg mL−1, the fluorescence of N, S-CQD @ MIP and N, S-CQD @ NIP composites gradually were quenched. But the quenching effect of N,S-CQDs@MIP were more remarkable than N,S-CQDs@NIP, because the specific cavities of N,S-CQDs@MIP towards Cur resulted in their higher quenching efficiency. Generally, the relationship between the concentration of Cur and the fluorescence quenching performance of N,S-CQDs@MIP can be expressed quantitatively using the Stern-Volmer equation.
F 0/F = K SV C Cur + 1
where F 0 and F represented the initial fluorescence and quenched fluorescence signal respectively, C Cur was the concentration of the Cur and K SV was the quenching constant.

The fluorescence quenching performance of N,S-CQDs@MIP at different Cur concentrations was linearly fitted under the best experimental conditions, the Linear Stern-Volmer relationships were observed for the N,S-CQDs@MIP and N,S-CQDs@NIP (Fig. 4 (a)) and both the N,S-CQDs@MIP and N,S-CQDs@NIP all showed linear Stern-Volmer relationships, where the K SV of the N,S-CQDs@MIP was 0.423 mL μg−1 (r2 = 0.996), and the corresponding K SV of the N,S-CQDs@NIP was 0.078 mL μg−1 (r2 = 0.993). The detection limit of the Cur was 1.357 nM, with a linear range of 0.339 μM–136.0 μM. Compared to other methods (Table 1 ), our N,S-CQDs@MIP demonstrated higher sensitivity and wider detection range for the detection of Cur.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability and the ability of anti-interference
ce:section.ce:para: The optical stability of the N,S-CQDs@MIP was evaluated by recording the fluorescence quenching performance F 0/F changes against reaction time after adding Cur. The result, as shown in Fig. 4(b), indicated that the react time between Cur and N,S-CQDs@MIP was short and remained stable fluorescence intensity in 1 h, which meant that the sensor of N,S-CQDs@MIP has superior photostability.

Meanwhile, the ability of anti-interference of the N,S-CQDs@MIP was evaluated by adding different medicines (Adriamycin hydrochloride, Quercetin, Tetracycline, Vancomycin injection, Docetaxel, Phenacetin, Artemisinin and Amoxicillin) into N,S-CQDs@MIP solution and the concentration of the above drugs (100 μg mL−1) were 10 times that of the Cur (10 μg mL−1). As shown in Fig. 4(c), due to the specific binding sites generated by molecular imprinting, the fluorescence quenching performance of N,S-CQDs@MIP remained slight vary even if the concentration of drugs were 10-fold that of Cur. These results confirmed the high selectivity of N,S-CQDs@MIP for Cur.

Besides, in order to further prove the anti-interference performance of the N,S-CQDs@MIP in complex environment, some interfering ions were added, including common ions (Na+, K+, Ca2+, HCO3 −, CO3 2−, Cl−, PO4 3−, HPO4 2−), protein (BSA), and the results shown in Fig. 4(d). No obvious effect could be observed, further supported the selectivity and specificity of the N,S-CQDs@MIP to Cur detection.

As shown in Fig. 5 (a), the absorption spectrum of Cur overlaped with the emission spectrum of N,S-CQDs obviously, indicated that the quenching mechanism of Cur on N,S-CQDs may be inner filter effect. On the other hand, the ultraviolet spectra of N, S-CQDs solution, Cur solution and the mixed solution of N, S-CQDs and Cur were recorded. As shown in Fig. 5(b), With the addition of Cur, the absorption peak of N, S-CQDs at 360 nm appears slight red shift and the characteristic absorption peak of Cur at 440 nm appears. From this, it can be inferred that Cur interacts with hydroxyl or carboxyl functional groups on the surface of N, S-CQDs to form a non-luminous complex. These observation demonstrated that the fluorescence quenching mechanism of Cur on N,S-CQDs was based on the static quenching [31].

In addition, the effect of temperature on the quenching constant K SV can also verify the quenching mechanism of Cur on N,S-CQDs. Stern-Volmer plots of F 0/F versus the concentration of Cur at three different temperatures (323 K, 298 K, 273 K) were depicted in Fig. 5(c). The quenching constants at the corresponding temperatures were 0.0031 mL μg−1, 0.0045 mL μg−1 and 0.0055 mL μg−1 respectively. With the increasing of temperature, the quenching constants decreased, further confirmed that the quenching mechanism was static quenching [14].

The fluorescence lifetimes measure further verify the quenching mechanism of Cur to N,S-CQDs, since for judging static quenching or dynamic quenching, the fluorescence lifetime is a key parameter. The presence of Cur not change fluorescence lifetime of N,S-CQDs while the dynamic quenching process reduces the fluorescence lifetime. Fig. 5(d) showed that the fluorescence decay spectra could be fitted using a double exponential curve (see supplementary material). The average fluorescence lifetime of N,S-CQDs was 13.24 ns, when the average fluorescence lifetimes of N,S-CQDs was 13.00 ns in the presence of Cur. There is no obvious fluorescence lifetime change indicated that the quenching mechanism of Cur was static quenching. Essentially, the fluorescence quenching mechanism of the interaction between N,S-CQDs and Cur is a synergy between static quenching and inner filter effect [15].
To test the feasibility of the N,S-CQDs@MIP to detect Cur in real sample analysis, we conducted the following two experiments:

For the determination of Cur in drugs, curcumin capsule was taken as the real sample and experimental result was shown in Table 2 . The Cur content in curcumin capsule was 82.7%–85.3% (w/w) which is in line with the 86% indicated in the instructions, and the recovery rate of the samples was 97.6–103.7%(n = 3). These results showes that the N,S-CQDs@MIP is capable of determining Cur simply in drugs. The simplicity of the workflow and easy accessibility of N,S-CQDs@MIP allows this analytical method to be used for online or at-site screening of Cur.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Content of Cur consumed by cells in 2 h
ce:section.ce:para: The application of N,S-CQDs@MIP was verified by the consumption of Cur by A549 cells within 2 h (Table S1). In this experiment, the consumed Cur by cells was obtained by the changes of Cur content before and after incubating for 2 h under the same cell conditions. With the increasing of Cur content, the Cur consumed by cells also increased, and this phenomenons were maintained after three repetitions with the same concentration. Therefore, the N,S-CQDs@MIP provides a good platform for the detection of Cur content consumed by cells.

In summary, a simple, rapid and low-cost fluorescence method for the Cur detection based on N,S-CQDs which prepared by simply mixing anhydrous ethylenediamine, l-cysteine and dehydrated trisodium citrate was built. The interaction between N,S-CQDs and Cur was determined to be a combined effect of inner filter effect and static quenching. Under the optimal conditions, good linearity was obtained in the concentration ranges of 0.339–136.0 μM and the corresponding LOD obtained was low as 1.357 nM. The detection of Cur in curcumin capsule was carried out by this method with the recovery rate of 97.6–103.7%, and the results were satisfactory, on the other hand, the N,S-CQDs@MIP for Cur detection in the cells of A549 was demonstrated that it is feasible to examine the content of Cur consumed by cells. Our proposed fluorescence method is simple and eases of operation, indicating the great potential for the ultra-sensitive detection of Cur.

Fang Fang: Completion of experiments, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Shaohua Zhang: Formal analysis. Chaofan Chen: Formal analysis, Formal analysis. Xueying Wang: Writing – review & editing, Data curation. Chuannan Luo: Funding acquisition, Formal analysis. Qin Wei: Funding acquisition, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was financially supported by the Innovation Team Project of Colleges and Universities in Jinan (No.2019GXRC027), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos.22204060), the Innovation Team Project of Colleges and Universities in Jinan (No.2020GXRC048).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.optmat.2023.113800.
